daily_fact: The U.S. Navy scheduled planes to fly over New York City on Thursday morning, giving residents across the five boroughs a chance to see a rare aerial display.
topline_md: New York City faced broad civic strain and change, with Con Edison cutting voltage to preserve reliability, a DOC captain indicted, rent and public defender funding tensions rising, and officials advancing school expansion, shelter Wi‑Fi, food aid, and housing-linked library rebuilds amid heated primary politics.

items.title: Con Edison Cuts Voltage 8% to Preserve Grid Reliability
items.why_it_matters: Reduced voltage can affect appliances and signals strain on the city’s electric system.

items.title: NYC plans new classrooms and hires to meet class-size law
items.why_it_matters: The city must add space and teachers, affecting school budgets, staffing, and students across neighborhoods.

items.title: Public defender unions warn of strike without more funding
items.why_it_matters: A strike could disrupt criminal court cases citywide and worsen delays for defendants and victims.

items.title: State lawmakers approve Wi-Fi requirement for homeless shelters
items.why_it_matters: Shelter residents would gain better access to jobs, schoolwork, benefits, and essential online services.

items.title: Rent Guidelines Board hearing erupts over proposed rent increases
items.why_it_matters: Any approved increase would directly raise costs for roughly one million rent-stabilized apartments.

items.title: Report finds $400 million in NYC homes vulnerable to deed theft
items.why_it_matters: Property owners, especially seniors, could lose homes through fraud without stronger city protections.

items.title: Six NYC libraries considered for rebuilds with affordable housing
items.why_it_matters: The plan could add housing and modern libraries, reshaping public land use in multiple neighborhoods.

items.title: Brooklyn safety redesign stalls as residents seek Third Avenue truck relief
items.why_it_matters: Delays leave a dangerous corridor unchanged for pedestrians, cyclists, and drivers in Sunset Park.

items.title: Correction captain indicted in alleged extortion and wage fraud scheme
items.why_it_matters: The case raises fresh concerns about corruption and oversight inside New York City's jail system.

items.title: Lawsuit challenges on-the-spot drug testing in NYC family courts
items.why_it_matters: The suit could change how parents are treated in custody and child welfare cases.

items.title: State offers $10 million to food banks and emergency food providers
items.why_it_matters: New funding could expand food access for New Yorkers facing hunger and rising grocery costs.

items.title: New Yorkers can apply for free air conditioners this summer
items.why_it_matters: Free cooling units can protect vulnerable residents from dangerous heat and lower summer hardship.

items.title: NYC to add new 465 area code
items.why_it_matters: Residents and businesses may soon see new phone numbers as demand for lines grows citywide.

items.title: Carroll Street Bridge reopens for pedestrians and cyclists only
items.why_it_matters: The restored bridge changes daily travel options and street access in Carroll Gardens and Gowanus.

items.title: Lower Manhattan opens Centre 360 public observatory
items.why_it_matters: The new free civic attraction adds public access and tourism activity near City Hall.

items.title: Chinese immigrants become NYC’s largest foreign-born group
items.why_it_matters: The demographic shift affects language access, schools, services, and political representation across the city.
